comment_teaching:
Although only 28 students enrolled this course, in each lecture and even tutorial session there were about 20 students attending, which is almost a miracle compared to other CS courses.

Both the professor and TAs were passionate and took their job seriously, and the classroom atmosphere was very good. They are really trying to teach students something in the proper way, and almost every student is willing to learn. This was a completely different experience from some courses where teachers only read ppts and students only do exam-oriented learning.
comment_workload: Not very high. Only 4 homework throughout the semester, but it may take some time thinking out the solution for students without competitive programming background or previous algorithm knowledge.
comment_grading:
Sunil has done his best to give us good grade, but because of the department's requirement and the high competitiveness in this course, the grade may be worse than 3711.

Maybe because I'm not as clever as my fellow students, I tried my best but only got B+. Despite this, I still highly recommend this course because university is not only about CGA and this course was really a rewarding experience for me.

comment_content: Very great and clear. I believe this course is pretty useful for undergraduate students in CSE.
comment_teaching: I like his style of typing and teaching at the same time, this helps me to understand the code and the content at the same time.
comment_workload: A little bit difficult but knowledgable, there are 4 assignments but each of them is challenge. Maybe need a lot of time after class for this course.
comment_grading: Very high and I got an A in this class, this is my first A actually and I appriciate it a lot.

comment_content: An overview of classic algorithms, with a stress on theory instead of coding. A little bit boring to me (that's why I only attended half of them).
comment_teaching: The professor is very nice and passionate, and likes to interact with students. I don't know about TAs.
comment_workload: Similar to the professor's 2711H, there are overall 4 homeworks and each have 8-10 problems. A lot of them are proofs. Each homework needs about 2-3 hours to complete. There are easy if you know those algorithms already, just pay attention to how to prove them correctly.
comment_grading: My final exam is totally forked up (lowest) but Istill got B. I think most people will get A in this course.

comment_teaching:
Arya is a passionate teacher and he can make you interested in the subject (that is, if the topic is not trivial enough).

He's is an amazing instruction if you like slow paced classes since he goes very slow and does not mind answering questions in the middle of the lecture.

He is also very cooperative and replies fast :)

Oh, TA is also nice but a bit of a harsh grader (in the sense that he finds mistakes if you dont mention trivial explanation).
comment_workload:
Basically 0 lmao.

There are 4 homeworks each with usually 8-10 questions, each which take time inversely proportional to your typing speed. I say this because the questions are not too hard and usually you can come up with the solution within a minute. You just need to type it down. For me, each homework took around 3-4 hours with homework 4 taking more time since I did not know flow algorithms so I had to learn that first.

It is really sad that this course is easy, because it was meant to be a challenging one.

comment_content:
Other comments have gone into great lengths on the syllabus of this year's COMP3711H, so I will only give a brief summary here, with some personal comments.

Before midterm:
- Overview on runtime analysis
- Basic data structures (heap, binary search tree, AVL trees)
- Graph algorithms (DFS, BFS, Dijskstra's, Bellman-Ford)
- Greedy algorithms (knapsack problem, scheduling)
- Dynamic programming
- Misc. (Partial sums, segment trees)

After midterm:
- Amirtized analysis (union of disjoint sets)
- String algorithms (tries, suffix trees, Ukkoken's, KMP)
- ...and some 3721 knowledge (DFAs and NFAs, regular languages, Turing completeness, NP-hardness and completeness)

...and these are just the main concepts. Many micellaneous algorithms were introduced along the way, especially in tutorials (btw, sidenote, if you are going to take Amir's 3711H, don't skip tutorials, they are very useful). Do not try your luck if you aren't prepared to spend at least 10 hours per week on this course, or you will die a horrible death. You have been warned.

Yet, if you are geninely curious on algorithms and ready to dedicate the time and effort, this course is suitable for beginners, despite the fast pace of the course.
comment_teaching:
Amir is quite good in teaching, but his pace is quite fast. You have to pay full attention in order to follow what he is saying, but there is a catch: the room for the lectures (Rm 5583) is quite stuffy, so it is very easy for you to fall asleep, especially when your brain is on turbo mode trying to understand the lecture content.

Also, the "tutorials" are actually just shorter lectures. They are taught by Amir and mostly cover homework content. TAs also hold tutorial sessions, but they are separate and occur outside official hours.
comment_workload:
If you are planning to take his 3711H, I will warn you again:

This course is not for the faint of heart.

I stole this quote from the home page for 2012H. Ironcially, 2012H was one of the easier courses I have taken. If we were to take the credit count of 2012H (5) at face value, this course would worth at least 10. Expect to spend at least 2 days for each homework and coding practices, which they are four each throughout the course.

One piece of advice: if you are struggling, just ask the TA. Don't misallocate your time and ignore other courses. I learnt this the hard way. 🫠
comment_grading:
Best grading I have ever seen in UST, hands down.

To give some context, I am pretty confident that my total score is below UQ. As Amir allowed for a one-time resubmission for each homework after TAs marked them, people worked crazy hard and the UQ for all of them were either full or near-full (~9.5-10/10). My homework scores were 8.25, 7.5, 9.5 and 10 respectively.

I didn't perform exceptionally in exams either. I was 0.5 points above UQ in midterm (18/30) and 1.5 points below UQ in final (18/30 again).

...and I got A+. I couldn't believe my eyes when I checked my grades on SIS. This means Amir must have given around 30% A+.

comment_content:
1. Overview: Roughly speaking the course gives a great coverage (roughly 90%) of the book CLRS, aka the most famous undergraduate-level algorithm textbook. Other good references are

- Prof. Golin's past 3711H notes, most of which are helpful for content before midterm. I guess there are still very few topics that the past 3711H covered (but not in this year) like fast Fourier transform and stable marriage problem. Speaking of Golin, I guess he is also keeping track of this course (even though he has left HKUST) since his name appears in the codeforce participation list.
- Prof. Dekai's past 271 notes. Although Dekai is notorious, still I realized his note is worth taking a look.
- Past 3711H exams are not quite helpful in overall since the style of questions are quite different. In particular, since Amir is a competitive programmer, the questions are designed in the respective way.

As a math and non-cs student, the most important takeaways for me are

i. Learn C++ - I guess this is one of the most significant distinction between this year's 3711H and its previous offering (also regular 3711), namely with providing C++ implementation of algorithms. As someone who didn't even take 2011 and knows nothing about C++, I find this course a good starting point to learn such a programming language.
ii. Recognize the difficulty of solving "discrete problems" - In fact many problems and settings for modern mathematics are made to be neat and too good to be true, so as to avoid dirty and technical difficulties.
(In particular, combinatorics plays a key role in some situations when we really choose to face dirty stuff. One example in my mind is the importance of combinatorics in random matrix theory and free probability, which comes from taking out "commutativity" which is taken for granted in standard situations like real line)
In the world of algorithms, the core is to face these difficulties. I would say my mathematical background doesn't quite help me a lot to understand the complicated course content (comparing with non-math students).

Speaking of prerequisites, I find most of us mentioned that 2711/2711H doesn't help a lot for the algorithm course. I would say I find discrete math really get involved in the following places:

i. Runtime analysis, especially the proof of Master's theorem, amortized analysis and so on
ii. Mathematical concepts and facts regarding graphs and trees
iii. Understanding the computation theory part, especially the 3-SAT problem
iv. A "mathematically-prepared mindset" for understanding the course content, especially in the dynamic programming part or do proof of correctedness using induction and so on

2. Course Content. I tend to divide the course in the following parts (not in teaching order):

===I. Runtime Analysis===

TL;DR: First foundation of algorithm analysis. The analysis of runtime can reflect the "efficiency" of algorithm.

- Basic definition of runtime: In this course the concept of space complexity is not as emphasized as runtime, although in some exam questions the space complexity is taken into consideration.
- Master theorem: Give a general formula to calculate runtime from a given form of recurrence relation
- Amortized analysis: In some case considering worst-case runtime is too "extreme" or "pessimestic", and this can reflect the "more realistic" runtime in some situations.

===II. Pure Array-sorting Algorithm===

TL;DR: Various classical sorting algorithm for arrays are introduced. This section is quickly gone through since graph algorithms are more important and efficient to implement, and graph algos are the main characters in this course. Without further conditions, it can be shown that the best possible runtime for array sorting must be O(n log n), so such kind of problem is somewhat "not interesting".

- Selection sort (Find the smallest number and switch to 1st position, then second smallest and switch to 2nd position and so on)
- Bubble sort ("Bubble up" the largest number to rightmost position, and so on)
- Mergesort (Divide the array into singletons, assemble the adjacent singletons while sorting)
- Quicksort (Find pivot, divide the array based on the pivot, do the same thing to the subarrays)
- Radix sort (Sort the array by looking at the last digit, second last,..., first digit of each number in the array)
- Divide and conquer (Generalization of idea of Mergesort; Should be treated as a methodology like DP)

===III. Basic Graph Algorithms===

TL;DR: Graph algos are more efficient, and many data structures are implemented using the idea of trees.

- Definition/facts of graphs, introduced in guest lecture
- Binary tree, BST, AVL tree
- Splay tree (Implemented in homework)
- DFS and BFS (Two fundamental graph-search algos; Super important!!!)
- Topological sort (Find underlying order in DAG)
- Dijkstra algorithm (Find shortest path)
- Tarjan algorithm (Detect strongly connected components)
- Minimum spanning tree and Kruskal algorithm (for finding MST)

===IV. Dynamic Programming===

TL;DR: As mentioned, DP is not a specific type of algo, but a general methodology like divide-and-conquer.  DP is so important that it can be applied in many situations and greatly reduce time complexity, but usually the correct scenario is usually difficult to be figured out.

- The general approach is to divide a problem into subproblems, and write down a recurrence relation and solve it mathematically. Of course speaking of recurrence relation, initial condition (base case) is essential.
- An essential tool for implementation is "memoization" which aims to reduce the number of unnecessary calls of recurrence function.
- Explicit examples: Prefix sums, Set covering, LCA...

===V. String Algorithms and Trees===

TL;DR: Algorithms for problems like string matching/common substring with trees.

- Segment trees (Label nodes with intervals)
- Tries/Prefix tree
- Suffix tree and Ukkonen's algorithm (for building suffix tree)

===VI. Computation Theory===

TL;DR: Discussion of highly theoretic topics like computability of problems, Turing machine, hardness/completeness and so on. The famous debate of P != NP or P=NP comes from this part.

- Regular expression (Homework)
- Halting problem and computability
- DFA and NFA; For those who have learnt stochastic processes, Markov chain is a good example of automata.
- Turing machine and Turing completeness (Important example: C++)
- P and NP problems, NP hard and NP complete
- Discussion on some NP-complete problems like 0-1 knapsack

===VII. Others (that I don't know how to classify)===

- Greedy algorithm which uses the idea of "local optimality". For those who have learnt some machine learning, gradient descent is a good example. Again this is a methodology like DP and divide-and-conquer, but not an explicit algo. Usually greedy algorithm is applied in optimization problem, just like the purpose of gradient descent.
- Huffman coding (An application of greedy algo)
- Randomization of algo, which plays a minor role in this course
- Some computational geometry (Homework)
- XOR problem and centroid decomposition, which applied some divide-and-conquer idea
- Sparse table
- Maximum flow and maxflow-mincut theorem
- Combinatorial game theory
comment_teaching:
I would say Amir is a combination of Desmond in CSE, and Ivan Ip in MATH. While Amir is willing to give many extra coding lectures (to implement algorithms introduced in class) like Desmond, he is able to maintain a quick pace of teaching and striving for mathematical rigor like the style of Ivan Ip.

All TAs contribute a lot for content like guidance of codeforce questions, and  enrichment of mathematical background like graphs. I do admire the efforts being made in this teaching team.
comment_workload:
There are multiple sources of workload which makes it undoubtedly worth a C in this scope:

1. My unfamiliarity on C++, which gives difficulty for me to follow the implementation of algorithms in the early stage of the course, and also tackling codeforce questions
2. Tremendous amount of course content - There are 46 regular lectures (coming from 3 lectures per week), plus weekly tutorial. I would absolute agree that the workload is moderate if this course is worth 5 or even 6 credits, but being 4 credit is too insulting for this course.
3. Codeforce questions: Although answers are always available on the internet, it is worth thinking for a while for some questions.
4. Homework problems: In fact there are some conceptual backgrounds of most homework problems, like splay tree in homework 1, or sweeping line method in homework 2 and so on. Without realizing these backgrounds, thing might be significantly more difficult.
5. Preparation of exam: Actually not everything should be prepared to be super familiar eventually, thanks to the flexibility of the exam. Precisely, you can choose 2 out of 3 big problems to answer in the exam paper, in order to completely avoid something you are not familiar with (like NP-completeness or amortized analysis in final exam).
comment_grading:
The grading is structured as 40% homework, 30% midterm and 30% final.

The exam papers are so difficult that, if you can perfectly finish 2 out of 6 (sub)problems plus being partially corrected in other subproblems, then you are able to be above mean and be safe in A-range. This is for midterm and I believe that there will not be a significant change for such grade distribution in final.

One thing that makes me a little bit unsatisfactory is that, there is a make-up exam for those who cannot attend midterm, but according to the paper released, the makeup paper seemed not significantly harder than the original one which I think causes unfairness.

comment_content: Very interesting course that gives you deep understanding of algorithms. You can walk out of the class with a lot more understanding on how to solve a lot of the challenging problems in leetcode. It is important to not stress yourself out in face of the challenging content. Actively reach out to TAs, professors, and peers in the class so that you can learn from them more effectively.
comment_teaching: Both the TAs and the professor's teaching are the best part about the course. Teaching is a little too fast pace in my opinion, leaving very little time for practicing and digesting the content until finals week. Thankfully we get two weeks to really spend time with the material before taking the final.
comment_workload: It takes time to learn the material. So make sure your schedule allows enough time for you to really dedicate to this class. Otherwise, you may find it hard to keep up with the pace. Coding practices and homework both take a long time to digest and complete.
comment_grading: Grading is rough because the homework and exams are hard.

comment_content:
The content is the same as before. You might pay some attention to the part that not covered before due to the different speed every year.

IMO, I think the content could be more since this is a 4-credit course.

comment_workload: The workload is only a normal 4-credit course's load, which is much lighter than an honor math course.

BTW, I heard that Golin gives a huge A&B range in the spring semester (3711).

comment_content:
( If you are a genius then don't read this post because I'm not one, especially when doing MATH )

And this post is subjective  :/

First of all, it teaches algorithm so don't come if you are a coding lover and is taking the honors COMP course for more programming exercise.

This is more a mathematical course than a COMP course ( at least when doing the assignments ), you are expected to write very strict and ( not too but still ) complicated proofs in assignments.

SO if you don't do well at MATH and especially hates writing proofs - well, take care :/  Getting start in writing a perfect proof and writing dozens of them hurts.

However, these contents are not hard to understand. BTW if you are not very into algorithms and proofs - take COMP3711 instead. The contents should be basically the same.
comment_teaching:
Nice professor, he teaches well but sometimes it gets boring ( sorry, my bad )

The course has a piazza for posting question like the usual COMP courses and sometimes professor gives hints about some of the assignment questions.
comment_workload:
(  I confess that I drift away when attending classes so I miss contents from time to time.  )

One can self-study the materials but it is also going to be tiring because they are long and full of proofs.

The assignments took a decade to finish. by finish I mean going into every details and double or even triple checking for correctness. On average, I take six hours to finish a single question if I study one topic from nothing.

Be prepared to become a study-holic (and even more than that) if you are also busy with other courses: I killed my weekends and most leisure events. ( never want to experience again )

However, sometimes you can't save them all even work to heart-attack: when coming to the  final p, you have loads of deadlines and have to review. Will you love a double-sized assignment? (  `w'  )

BUT, if you have a plenty of time, come ahead, it is well-worth taking. At the end of this course you will be okay with MATH proofs, even though you might still dislike them.

And try to finish the tutorial questions before attending the tutorial session.






( Even recalling is tiring )
comment_grading:
This year no midterm and the final exam is not a difficult one, as long as you are familiar with the algorithms and definition you are going to manage it.

You can improve you grade by doing some bonus questions, but it won't be useful if you are not on the cut-off edge.

He mentioned that you are guaranteed a A if overall grade is >=90, B if >=80. Besides,  he said around 90% of the students will get A or B while more are getting A.

But bear in mind that it is still competitive & time-consuming as honor course attracts people with golden medals :/

Anyway, grading is fair. If you are not a genius but want to challenge yourself, make sure you have time and energy for it.

sorting, BST, randomized algorithm, divide&conquer, graph, dynamic programming, max flow, hashing.

The focus is more on mathematical proofs of each algorithm than how to use them for specific problems. I would've appreciated more if some more practical usages of algorithms were included in the course..
comment_teaching: He is passionate, and a good teacher I think. He sometimes makes mistakes, but it is understandable. Also, he is a little bit strict on formality (of your proof). He replies to questions super fast, which is great.
comment_workload: The HW takes freakishly long to finish. I mean, workload wise, it almost felt like 2012H, a 5 credit course, or even worse.
comment_grading:
Honestly, Prof. Golin seems like he would grade very strictly. However, I think grading scheme is quite lenient: it is hard to get full mark as he emphasizes on getting all the details correct too, but in fact he does not penalize you for the mistakes too much. Also, if there are multiple parts of questions (and there will always be multiple parts for one question), each subpart will be graded separately for the most part: you won't lose much point for the second part because you got wrong in the first part.

This semester was entirely online, so no MT, but 6 assignments, 1 final. The last assignment was double sized, so essentially 7 assignments.

HW 60% mean: 94/100
Final 40% mean: 82/100

It is an H course so the class is quite competitive.

One good thing of the course is that since it is an H course, some absolute grading is applied. If you get >90%, you are guranteed an A-, and B- gurantee for >80%, and so on. In practice, a little below 90% would still get you A- or even A (depending on the competitiveness of the class)

comment_content:
"The course is running BFS but not DFS."

This course covers lots and lots of algorithmic contents. From basic greedy algorithms, to advanced graph algorithms such as max flow. The content covered is mostly the same as the non-honor version, but with more proofs.

Due to the wide range of content, most of them are not discussed in depth. Even worse, for those the topics with depth, you don't have time to digest the topic before moving on to the next one. I think it would be better to have fewer topics with more examples and techniques.

Moreover, the course is supposed to put more focus on the tools of proofs, but not skimming through algorithms. The course just simply assume us to knowing how to write a mathy proof in advance. I believe the problem is the key steps of the proofs are not highlighted during lecture. So we will easily get stuck or end up with a proof that is not rigorous enough.

It would be much better if this course can be splitted into two separate course. One is for introduction to the algorithms and the other one is advanced algos/data structures with analysis. The latter one should include things like basic reductions or some simple complexity theories. This is an honor course and it supposes to introduce things that are more difficult.
comment_teaching:
I personally not a fan of golin's teaching style and most of the reviews are a little bit overrated in my opinion. The slides are lengthy with too much details. He claimed you can learn all the things from the slides without attending the lecture. It turns out he tend to talk a lot during lecture without emphasizing the main points. You will get lost easily if you do not have some prior knowledge.

Nonetheless, I do feel his passion for the class. He did put quite a bit of effort into the class arrangements. Detailed instructions for course assessments are given. For the online exam on Feb, he sent out many emails and did test runs to ensure our setup run smoothly. The solution of homework and exams were provided in details with marking notes. You even have to send a special form for appealing. I sometimes feel he over-complicated many things....
comment_workload:
Considering this is an honor course, the workload is reasonable.

The time-consuming part is the assignments. Writing up nice and detailed proofs take a lot of time. For me, each question takes me about 3 hours to complete while there are 4-5 questions in each assignment. Also, think twice before you submit. You may miss some small important parts that could mess up your whole proof.  

Both midterm and exam are closed-book. However, the questions are quite easy that they are slightly modified versions of the past paper and class notes. Be sure to do some practice with past paper or you may get below mean like me. :C Really regret I did not recite the answers of the past papers before the midterm. I lost most of the points in one of the directly copied from past paper question. So, in terms of revision, the only work would be reviewing the lecture notes and do past papers which would not be too much workload.

Attendance in lectures or tutorials are not mandatory. As mentioned above, you can learn all the things from the slides without attending the lecture. I attended most of the lectures but not the tutorials. Don't expect too much from the course TAs......
comment_grading:
It feels like you can easily get below mean in assignments and exams because this class is quite competitive. Despite my good algorithms background (being in competitive programming team and knew most of the algos in advance), I will still get a bad result if I do not work hard.

My score and class stats:
Assignment 1: 91/100 (Mean: 94.2)
Assignment 2: 100/100 (Mean: 96.4)
Assignment 3: 95/100 (Mean: 90.1)
Assignment 4: 94/100 (Mean: 60.1, probably due to P/F scheme)
Assignment total: 380/400 => 28.5/30
Midterm: 79/100 (Mean: 80, Median: 80, Max: 98) => 23.7/30
Final: 94/100 (Stats not released) => 37.6/40
Extra Credit Problems: 13/16
Total: 89.8/100 => A (A- without EC)

Prof. sent me a email saying that my grade was just below the A threshold, and I would receive an A instead of A- because of the EC problems. I'm not sure but I guess this course uses absolute grading with a 90 A cutoff.
